1: A new divine speech introduces the rod-test — YHWH's definitive authentication of the Aaronic priesthood following the Korah rebellion. The timing is significant: the rod-test comes immediately after the plague of Numbers 16:41-50, while the people are still living with the memory of the earth-swallowing and fire and 14,700 dead. The rod-test will provide a permanent visual sign rather than a repeated verbal command.
2: YHWH instructs Moses to collect twelve staffs (mattot) from all the leaders of Israel — one per ancestral house (bet av), twelve representing the twelve tribes. The matteh (staff/rod) is both the symbol of tribal leadership and a measuring/counting implement. The choice of the staff as the test object is significant: it is the dead wood of authority, capable of producing new growth only if YHWH acts.
3: uchtavta shem ish al matteihu: 'Write each man's name on his staff.' The names inscribed on the staffs make the test individually attributable and prevent later disputes about which staff belonged to whom. Aaron's name is specifically written on Levi's staff — the priestly claim is personal and tribal. When the staff buds, Aaron's name is on it: the authentication is both individual and institutional.
4: The staffs are placed 'before the covenant document' (lifney ha'edut) — in the most holy place, before the ark. The ark contains the covenant tablets; placing the staffs before the covenant document aligns the authentication procedure with the covenant's own authority. The twelve staffs before YHWH's covenant is the overnight divine jury: YHWH deliberates through the night and returns his verdict in the morning in the form of living growth.
5: hayah ha'ish asher avchar bo mattehu yifrah: 'The man I choose — his staff will sprout.' The test's logic is elegant: a dead wooden staff cannot sprout unless YHWH acts. The sprouting is not metaphorical but botanical — the text specifies flowers, almonds, and new growth. YHWH's choice is not communicated through vision or voice but through botany: the order of creation responds to the Creator's word in a way that cannot be faked. The test also frames the issue as divine election (asher vacharthi = whom I have chosen) rather than human appointment.
6: Moses collects the twelve staffs; the leaders give their staffs willingly. The entire leadership structure of Israel participates in the test: every tribal leader submits his staff to overnight divine judgment. This is the ultimate democratic test — not twelve competing claims adjudicated by Moses's word, but twelve dead sticks laid before YHWH simultaneously.
7: vayannach Moshe et hamaттот lifney YHWH be'ohel ha'edut: 'Moses placed the staffs before YHWH in the tent of the covenant.' The act of placement is the full extent of human agency in the test — Moses puts the staffs in the right place and steps back. What happens next is entirely YHWH's action. This is the model of authentic ministry: bring people and objects to the right place before YHWH, and let YHWH's own act be the authenticating sign.
8: vayhi mimacharat vayavo Moshe el ohel ha'edut vehineh parach matteh Aharon lebeit Levi: 'The next day Moses went into the tent of the covenant, and there was Aaron's staff — it had budded, produced blossoms, and borne ripe almonds.' The triple botanical progression — parach (budded/sprouted), vayatsets (put forth flowers), vayyigmal (bore ripe fruit) — compresses the full growth cycle of an almond tree (weeks to months) into a single overnight. The almond (shaqed) has a secondary significance: shaqed comes from shaqad (to watch/be alert) — the almond is the 'watching tree,' the first to bloom in spring. YHWH uses the 'watching tree' to watch over his choice.
9: Moses brings all twelve staffs out before the whole congregation. Every Israelite can see the result: eleven dead sticks and Aaron's living, flowering, fruiting staff. The visual contrast is the verdict. The congregation's response ('they looked, and each man took his staff') is muted — there is no recorded grumbling or challenge. The botanical miracle is unanswerable.
10: hashev et matteh Aharon lifney ha'edut lemishmerет le'ot livney meri: 'Put Aaron's staff back before the covenant document, to be kept as a sign against the rebels.' The command to preserve the staff permanently establishes it as an ongoing sign (ot) and warning (lemishtemirat = as a preserved thing). The budding staff joins the jar of manna and the covenant tablets in the covenant chest — three objects preserved before YHWH as testimony to specific acts of grace and judgment in the wilderness. Hebrews 9:4 lists all three items inside the ark.
11: Moses's obedience is noted with the standard compliance formula. The brevity of this verse after the dramatic overnight miracle creates a narrative decompression: YHWH spoke, Moses obeyed, the sign is in place. No further divine elaboration is needed — the budding staff is self-explanatory as divine authentication of Aaronic election.
12: hinnenu govim hafnu kulanu avadnu: 'We are dying, we are perishing, we are all lost.' The Israelites' response to the authentication miracles of chapters 16-17 is terror rather than confidence. The three synonymous expressions of destruction (gava, avad, tam = died, perished, finished) pile up in increasing despair. The very demonstrations of YHWH's holiness that authenticate the priestly system have also revealed the danger of YHWH's presence to the unprepared. The question the people pose at the end of the verse ('anyone who comes near the tabernacle shall die — are we all going to die?') is genuine theological crisis.
13: kol haqorev haqorev el mishkan YHWH yamut: 'Anyone who comes near the tabernacle of YHWH shall die — are we all going to die?' This despairing question is the theological climax of the Korah-to-staff sequence. The people have now seen: unauthorized approach to the sanctuary kills. Their question anticipates the NT's great high priest theology: if YHWH's holiness is genuinely lethal to the sinful, how can any sinner approach? The answer Numbers 18 provides is the priestly mediation system; the answer Hebrews provides is Christ, the permanent high priest who is 'holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners, and exalted above the heavens' (Hebrews 7:26), who opens access rather than closing it.
